pattern_recognition: Binary Search is a classic algorithmic technique used for searching in sorted arrays. It is characterized by its divide-and-conquer approach, where the search space is halved with each iteration. Signals include the presence of a sorted array and the need for efficient searching.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Iterate through each element in the array and check if it matches the target.
approaches.algorithm: Start from the first element and compare each element with the target until the target is found or the end of the array is reached.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Utilize the properties of a sorted array to eliminate half of the search space with each comparison.
approaches.algorithm: Initialize two pointers, low and high, to the start and end of the array. Calculate the mid index and compare the middle element with the target. Adjust the pointers based on the comparison until the target is found or the pointers converge.

dry_run: Given the input array nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] and target = 3. Start with low = 0 and high = 4. Calculate mid = (0 + 4) / 2 = 2. nums[2] is 3, which matches the target. Return index 2.
edge_cases: An empty input array should return -1. A single element array should return 0 if the element matches the target, or -1 if it does not. Duplicates in the array should return the index of any occurrence of the target. Large input arrays should be handled efficiently without exceeding time limits. Overflow should be considered when calculating mid index.
common_pitfalls: 1) Forgetting to handle the case when the target is not present, leading to incorrect results. 2) Incorrectly calculating the mid index, which can lead to infinite loops or missed targets. 3) Not considering edge cases like empty arrays or arrays with a single element.
